text: Dutch farmer escapes international uncertainty. [Sector forecast](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/index.html). [Agricultural](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/agrarisch/index.html). 20/06/2025 • 4 minutes reading.
The American import tariffs create uncertainty on international markets, although Dutch farmers notice little of this.

Domestic affairs such as the fall of the Schoof cabinet and the National Termination Scheme for Livestock Farming Locations require full attention. View the agricultural sector forecasts in 2 minutes. In this video, sector economist Melanie Murk talks in 2 minutes about the growth expectations in agriculture for 2025 and 2026. See what challenges the agricultural sector faces and what you can do as an entrepreneur. [Go to the video](/nl/business/insights/sectors-and-trends/sectorprognoses/agricultural/domestic-developments-questions-all-attention-van-farmers-and-gardeners.html).

The scheme causes a significant drop in volume in the animal sectors. For the entire agricultural sector, ABN AMRO expects a volume decline of 2.5 percent in 2025, although results at company level remain favorable. Dutch livestock farming is in a phase of livestock shrinkage. The government has received 1,578 applications for the National Termination Scheme for Livestock Farming Locations (Lbv(+)). Farmers who accept the government subsidy will stop their livestock farming or will soon stop it. The number of chickens, pigs and to a lesser extent cows will therefore decrease. A decrease in the supply of meat, dairy and eggs creates upward price pressure. Not an unnecessary luxury, because costs have also risen considerably in recent years. The fall of the cabinet is extremely unfortunate in terms of timing.

It is still unclear to what extent the nitrogen plans presented can be implemented. The starter package presented in April contains the outlines of a solution to remove the Netherlands from the nitrogen lock.

However, the plan requires further elaboration and still lacks budgetary approval in important parts. A clear perspective for agricultural and horticultural companies will once again disappear from view in the absence of decision-making. Persistent uncertainty puts a significant brake on entrepreneurs' willingness to invest, causing business innovation and sustainability to come to a standstill.

In this context, ABN AMRO recently published the Pragmatic Approach to Nitrogen, an update! [Read also: Pragmatic approach to nitrogen crisis, an update](/nl/business/insights/sectors-and-trends/agricultural/pragmatic-approach-nitrogencrisis-an-update.html).

The impact of the Lbv can be positive for arable farmers. Cessation of livestock farmers frees up agricultural land that, if suitable, may be used for arable farming. ABN AMRO therefore expects an increase in the arable area from 2026. In addition, the drought has so far had little negative impact on crop growth, with the exception of plots that cannot be irrigated.

Prices fluctuate sharply in the first half of 2025. For example, the prices of consumption potatoes have fallen sharply since February, while the prices of yellow onions rose during the same period. In the short term, ABN AMRO expects that reducing the use of chemical crop protection products will put pressure on yields. Precision agriculture, green crop protection products and integrated pest management (Integrated Crop Management) can reduce this pressure, provided that techniques are available to entrepreneurs quickly enough. On the one hand, production in greenhouse horticulture is going well because of the sunny spring. On the other hand, the sector does face challenges that make production more difficult, such as the use of new resistant varieties and a decrease in the use of chemical crop protection products.
Floriculture is generally having a good start to the year.

Despite [positive results](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/media/Rapport-Agravia-250423_tcm16-261352.pdf) at company level and an increase in the area in vegetable sectors, ABN AMRO expects a decrease in volume of the entire agricultural sector. This decline, of 2.5 percent in 2025 and 2 percent in 2026 respectively, is mainly driven by shrinkage of the livestock as a result of the LBV(+).

Export volumes to the US limited. Only 0.1 percent of Dutch dairy and meat and 0.2 percent of fruit and vegetables grown in the Netherlands are exported to the United States (US). The consequences of the import tariffs are therefore hardly noticeable for the Dutch farmer. In floriculture, dependence on the US is much higher at 2.3 percent. In 2023, the sector exported 285 million euros to the country.

This mainly concerns tulips, daffodils and hyacinth bulbs. Furthermore, 'secondary' companies that supply Dutch exporters or directly to the US are affected, such as breeders and seed suppliers of flowers, plants and vegetables, and parties that focus on animal genetics and hatching eggs. Conversely, Dutch agriculture mainly imports soy and corn from the US for animal feed. According to Statistics Netherlands (CBS), roughly 30 percent of our soy comes from the US. However, percentages can vary considerably from year to year due to changes in supply and quality worldwide.

If negotiations between the US and EU fail, it is likely that the EU will take countermeasures aimed at soy and corn. Because these involve agricultural raw materials, it is relatively easy to purchase products elsewhere, for example from Brazil or Argentina. But because we are highly dependent on the US at European level, the prices of soy and corn from other countries can still rise sharply if all European member states want to purchase outside the US at the same time. If these countries prove to be unable to supply enough, they will still have to purchase from the US. A countermeasure will therefore be accompanied by a price increase for European livestock farmers. At the same time, the countermeasure is an incentive for European arable farmers to increase soy production. Eurostat figures show that Europe currently produces only 43.2 percent of its soy meal needs and 18.6 percent of soy beans itself.

Read all the facts and developments in the report '[Dutch sectors highly dependent on the United States](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/sectorprognoses/alle-sectoren/nederlandse-sectoren-sterk-dependent-van-verenigde-staats.html)'.

Many countries and organizations therefore see the Netherlands as an agricultural guide country. With an export of more than EUR 100 billion, Dutch agriculture and horticulture makes a strong contribution to our economy.
